replies.author: Dr. Lei
replies.content:
Very good. Just two comments:


1. You only need to include Threat Model & Motivation in one response, and then reference it in other responses. This will save you quite some space, which you can use to respond to other comments.
2. Good job on Threat Model & Motivation, which is a strong response to the relevant comments.

replies.author: Deactivated User
replies.content: Thank you for the feedback, Professor. I actually wasn't aware that referencing responses across reviewers was an option, it will definitely help manage the word count. I will include the Threat Model and Motivation into a single core response and then reference it for the other reviewers.

replies.author: Dr. Lei
replies.content:
Very good. A few comments:


1. For the threat model, try to explicitly spell out the differences between the Curious-but-Honest adversary paradigm. The reviewers are likely unfamiliar with this paradigm. The point you want them to appreciate is that (a) the CBH paradigm is established by existing work; and (b) the differences between yours and the CBH paradigm are reasonable, i.e., they do not invalidate the paradigm.
2. Also try to be very clear about the two scenarios, i.e., Collaborative Inference and Safety Auditing. Try not to mix them up, which can be confusing.
3. When you respond to "misunderstandings", try to be explicit about what "misunderstandings" you refer to, if the space allows.

replies.author: Deactivated User
replies.content: Thank you, Professor, for the feedback. I will incorporate your suggestions.

replies.author: Dr. Lei
replies.content: Samreen, one more suggestion as you finalize the response: Prioritization is really important to deal with the 250-word limit. When you must choose, it is better to respond to critical comments clearly while ignoring less critical comments, than trying to respond to all comments but not clearly to the most critical comments. You can mention this at the top of the response, something like, we will only respond to the most critical comments due to space limitation.

replies.author: Deactivated User
replies.content: Sure, Professor. I will prioritize the most critical comments to stay within the word limit as you suggested. I’ll send you the final version for a quick look before I submit today

replies.author: Deactivated User
replies.content:
Hi Professor, I’ve finalized the responses for all three reviewers. I focused heavily on the critical comments regarding the threat model.
Could you please take a look when you have a moment? Once you’ve reviewed, I’ll submit them.
[Review #249A](https://submit.petsymposium.org/2026.4/review.php/249A)**:**
**Datasets:** We have completed additional experiments on XSum and observed consistent trends, which we will include in the revision. While news-focused, XSum is the established benchmark for Instruction-Based Anonymized Summarization [Hughes, EMNLP 2025] providing 226k professional abstractive summaries. Access to authentic clinical/legal datasets is restricted by privacy regulations, and synthetic versions often lack the linguistic complexity necessary for auditing internal model representations. We will discuss the generalizability of our findings to high-stakes domains in the revision.
**Threat Model & Motivation:** We clarify that our threat model focus on Intermediate States (ISs) during active inference, which requires no long-term or expensive activation storage.
While LLMs are increasingly integrated into daily routines, their massive scale (e.g., Llama-3-405B) prohibits fast local inference, forcing users to send raw prompts to cloud and risking privacy leakage. To address this, recent research proposes Collaborative Inference to enforce data locality [Dong, USENIX 2025]: shallow layers are executed on a local device, and only Internal States (ISs) are transmitted to cloud to complete inference. Furthermore, to meet trustworthy AI requirements, these ISs may be exposed to third parties for safety auditing (identifying factual errors or jailbreaks). We adapt standard Curious-but-Honest adversary, like a malicious third-party auditor, who can stealthily intercept these transient ISs.
We evaluate Instruction-Based Anonymized Summarization, building on [Hughes, EMNLP 2025]. We will revise the manuscript to center the threat on ISs in Collaborative Inference, clarifying why an auditor can intercept ISs during inference without needing raw input or stored memory.

[Review #249B](https://submit.petsymposium.org/2026.4/review.php/249B)
** ***Note: Due to space, we focused on most critical concerns; all other points will be addressed in the revision.*
** Threat Model & Motivation:** While LLMs are increasingly integrated into daily routines, their massive scale (e.g., Llama-3-405B) prohibits fast local inference, forcing users to send raw prompts to cloud and risking privacy leakage. To address this, recent research proposes Collaborative Inference to enforce data locality [Dong, USENIX 2025]: shallow layers are executed on a local device, and only Internal States (ISs) are transmitted to cloud to complete  inference. Furthermore, to meet trustworthy AI requirements, these ISs may be exposed to third parties for safety auditing ( identifying factual errors or jailbreaks). We adapt standard Curious-but-Honest adversary, like a malicious third-party auditor, who can stealthily intercept these transient ISs.
We evaluate Instruction-Based Anonymized Summarization, building on inference-time redaction paradigm [Hughes, 2025, EMNLP]. Modern pipelines leverage LLMs to summarize and anonymize simultaneously. Redaction site t* provides a critical privacy diagnostic: identity recovery at t* reveals mechanistic alignment failure. Even with safe output ([PERSON]), internal computation fails to suppress identity during instruction execution. This decoupling proves a fundamental instruction following failure.
**Q0:** Teacher Forcing ensures ISs are extracted and evaluated at the same token positions, enabling a mathematically consistent audit at t* and t-1.
**Q1: **Identity subspaces are task-specific. For identity-required scenario, we train separate probes on scenario-specific states. Constructing subspaces from these probe weights isolates unique signals; we do not expect similarity as instructions significantly alter internal representations.
* *
* *
 [Review #249C](https://submit.petsymposium.org/2026.4/review.php/249C)
 **1. Architecture & Task Clarification:** There is a fundamental misunderstanding regarding our model. We do not use a RoBERTa-based NER model for redaction. We evaluate Instruction-Based Anonymized Summarization, building on the inference-time redaction paradigm [Hughes, EMNLP 2025]. Modern pipelines leverage LLMs to summarize and anonymize simultaneously. We used NER strictly to identify names in dataset summaries and replace them with [PERSON] to create ground-truth redacted summaries for experiments; it was never part of the model's redaction mechanism.
 **2.** **Threat Model & Motivation:** We evaluate Instruction-Based Anonymized Summarization, building on inference-time redaction paradigm [Hughes, EMNLP 2025]. While LLMs are increasingly integrated into daily routines, their massive scale (Llama-3-405B) prohibits fast local inference, forcing users to send raw prompts to cloud and risking privacy leakage. To address this, recent research proposes Collaborative Inference to enforce data locality [Dong, USENIX 2025]: shallow layers execute locally, and only Internal States (ISs) are transmitted to cloud to complete inference. Furthermore, to meet trustworthy AI requirements, these ISs may be exposed to third parties for safety auditing (identifying factual errors or jailbreaks). We adapt standard Curious-but-Honest adversary like a malicious third-party auditor who can stealthily intercept these transient ISs.
3. **Applicability:** We acknowledge framing regarding "activation logging, model inspection" was imprecise. We will revise manuscript to center threat on ISs in Collaborative Inference. This clarifies why a malicious auditor can intercept ISs during inference without needing access to raw input prompt or stored memory. We will perform a manual revision to improve clarity throughout manuscript.

replies.author: Deactivated User
replies.content:
Dear Professor,
Following our discussion, I have formalized the threat model for our work, rooting it in the established **Curious-but-Honest** paradigm found in recent privacy literature (e.g., *Depth*, USENIX 2025; *Neural Breadcrumbs*, EACL 2026).
I have specifically highlighted the **Collaborative Inference** scenario where the input stays local while the adversary handles **transient Internal States (ISs)** in-memory. This allows us to frame our work as a diagnostic for **"Mechanistic Alignment"**—checking if the model's internal logic actually follows its redaction instructions at the specific site t*.

Could you please review the draft below for the Threat Model section?
**3. Threat Model**
We define a realistic threat model based on the **Curious-but-Honest** adversary paradigm established in recent privacy-enhancing technology (PET) literature [Depth, 2025; Neural Breadcrumbs, 2026].

**3.1 Adversary Profile**
We consider a privileged adversary, such as a **Cloud Compute Provider** in a collaborative inference setting or a **Third-party Safety Auditor** [Mölander et al., 2023].


- **Collaborative (Split) Inference:** To ensure **data locality**, a client (e.g., a hospital or legal firm) runs the initial layers of the model—including the sensitive embedding layer—on a local, trusted device. Only the resulting **Internal States (ISs)** are offloaded to the cloud-based adversary to complete the deep-layer computation. The adversary is technically and contractually restricted from accessing the raw input text.
- **Safety Auditing:** The adversary is authorized to inspect internal representations to verify model behavior (e.g., auditing for toxicity) but is not granted access to the underlying sensitive prompt data.


**3.2 Adversary Knowledge (White-box)**
Consistent with "Insider Threat" scenarios, we assume a **White-box** setting where the adversary has access to the model weights. While the adversary only intercepts the **transient ISs** during the live forward pass, their knowledge of the model's parameters allows them to train sophisticated probes to map these abstract vectors back to original PII.

**3.3 Adversary Goal: Auditing Instruction-Logic Alignment** While prior work on IS inversion focuses on general semantic recovery [Depth, 2025], our adversary specifically aims to exploit failures in **Mechanistic Alignment**. The goal is to recover **Personally Identifiable Information (PII)** at the **redaction-aligned site t***. This focus allows us to evaluate a specific, high-stakes vulnerability: whether the model's internal compute layer aligns with its privacy-preserving instructions. Recovering an identity at t* proves that the model’s internal representation remains "toxic" despite a successful surface-level redaction in the output text.

3.4 Adversary Capacities
The attack is **passive** and requires **zero persistent storage**. The adversary simply intercepts the transient vectors that are **resident in GPU memory** as a functional requirement of the forward pass. Because the vectors must exist in memory to calculate the next layer's output, the adversary "snoops" this data in real-time without incurring the overhead of long-term storage or brute-force search.

replies.author: Deactivated User
replies.content: **Auditor context:** the **Mokander et al. (2023) [Auditing large language models: a three-layered approach. *****AI and Ethics*****]** reference is to explain that auditors inspect hidden states to verify safety/redaction behavior under regulations like the **EU AI Act**.

replies.author: Deactivated User
replies.content:
I have drafted a rebuttal response for Reviewer B that addresses almost all of their comments and questions while staying within the strict 250-word limit.
To save space, I integrated the responses to the primary weaknesses (W1 and W2) regarding privacy motivation into **Q0**. I also combined the technical rebuttals for **C1** (the "white-elephant" theory) and **C2** (linearity) within the response for **Q1**.

Could you please check the draft below
**Weaknesses:**
My primary concern with this paper is its applicability to PoPETs. The privacy motivation is poorly justified. The work would be better served by a venue on ML or maybe even on ML interpretability. As relevance is somewhat subjective in this case, this is a low confidence review.
- W1. Privacy applications and motivations are somewhat forced. The motivation presents a scenario
  where some LLM monitoring system would record model activations which could inadvertently record
  private information. The scenario becomes more convoluted the more I think about it:
  - The monitoring system did not record the prompts which contains the PII in the plain? What kind
    of LLM monitoring system records internal activations but not the prompts? If the prompts are
    recorded, the privacy issues are a problem regardless of activations.
  - Assuming the system operates in a sensitive setting where prompts are indeed not recorded but
    activations are. Even here, the activations over the part and subsequent parts of the input
    that has the PII would contain the sensitive information. Activations can be inverted and
    trivially so from early layer. So this must be a system that decided prompts should not be
    stored because they are sensitive but decided to store activations which, depending on the
    layer, store at least the same information.
  - Maybe the monitoring system does not record activations from the prompt and only the
    activations (and maybe even the actual tokens) of the response? This would make more sense but
    the focus on the [NAME] token or prior to it suggests that this is not a concern. That is, if
    the concern was response activations, why only care about activations near the redaction point?
  - Redaction is not typically done by auto-regressive models. Bi-directional models such as BERT
    and related used as token classifiers are used for this.
  - G1. Suggestions: First consider more interpretability or ML-focused venues where an
    investigation of the mechanisms of redaction would be more appreciated. Alternatively a more
    nuanced framing of why such an investigation is relevant to PoPETs should be included. Also, at
    least one of the points above could be addressed by expanding the scope of search across the
    entire prompt/response instead of just nearby the redaction. The last point can also be
    addressed by investigating bi-directional models.
- W2. Focus on just the local span near the [NAME] generation is limited. In the scope of a
  monitoring system for example, knowing that the private information is in these hidden states is
  not meaningful if the information is also in the other hidden states (whether prompt or
  response).
  - G2. Suggestions: My suggestion to W1 above already covers this: investigate the whole response
    (and even the prompt) for presence of the identity information. This could also help connect
    better to a more justifiable privacy-enhancing mechanism for the activation monitor (one that
    removes identity from all activations logged).
**Other comments/questions:**
- C1. I could see the task and specifics of redaction instructing having a big impact on model's
  internal representation and thus results of the work. Redaction may be a "white-elephant" kind of
  scenario where a model is induced to think about something more so than they would if they were
  not instructed to not think about it (I'm extrapolating here a bit about what "thinking" means).
  Consider testing only the redaction task without summarization and other tasks.
- C2. Can the private information be recoverable but not linearly so?
- C3. The paper could use some focused take-aways from the various angles of analysis. At least
  consider highlighting the most important observations.

**Areas to address in rebuttal response**
- Q0. Is the response indicative of any misunderstanding of the work? If so, please elaborate.
  Possibly related: is "teacher forcing" just the intervention/steering or does that refer to not
  ignoring its generations and using existing response tokens? (or something else?)
- Q1. Section 4.4 mentions a case where an LLM needs to output the identity instead of redacting.
  Some interventions are then done on the identity subspace. Was this the same identity subspace
  discovered under the summarize but redact scenario (i.e. instruction prompt) or was it another
  one trained in this scenario/prompt? Are they expected to be similar either way? See also C1.

**Response to Reviewer #249B**
**Q0 (Misunderstandings):** We evaluate Instruction-Based Inference-time Anonymized Summarization [Hughes, 2024] within a Privacy-Preserving Collaborative Inference framework [Dong, 2025]. The client maintains data locality; only transient Internal States (ISs) are offloaded to a Curious-but-Honest server (e.g., Cloud Provider or Auditor [Mökander, 2023]), strictly restricted from raw prompts. The redaction site t* is our privacy diagnostic: recovery here proves a mechanism failure where representations remain privacy-compromising despite anonymization commands. We use Teacher Forcing to "freeze" the sequence path, ensuring states are evaluated at identical token coordinates across samples for a mathematically consistent privacy audit.
**Q1:** Subspaces were learned separately for each scenario. For "Identity Required" baseline (Section 4.4), the instruction is to "Summarize the article." We evaluate the model as it predicts the true name by passing original summary under Teacher Forcing. We extract states at t-1 to map identity representation when functionally necessary. Using a separate subspace from this standard task ensures our intervention targets identity data itself without damaging the model’s instruction-following logic. Regarding C1/C2, "Redact-Only" is an inadequate substitute as it only tests static string replacement. Our setup evaluates Anonymized Summarization, where the model must utilize input context while obeying output privacy rules. Our causal results via re-evaluating post-intervention rebut the "white-elephant" theory: removing identity signal at t-1 does not stop redaction. Furthermore, as transformer computation is largely linear, non-linearly recoverable PII is effectively unusable by subsequent layers. Thus, linear probes establish a robust lower bound for usable privacy risk. We will clarify these points in the revision.
